current_flagged_state: EnsureCollege places a small uppercase, letter-tracked Space Mono 'eyebrow' label directly above nearly every section heading on the site, including the hero. The label is decorative/categorical (e.g. a generic section name) rather than content-specific, and it repeats in identical size/weight/spacing before each H2-level block. This was flagged as a moderate AI tell because 'a tiny uppercase letter-spaced label sitting immediately above an oversized hero headline... is the default AI SaaS hero,' and 'repeating tiny uppercase tracked labels above section headings turns a brand page into AI editorial scaffolding' (impeccable.style/slop). At the same time, the mono-label-over-serif-heading pairing is a load-bearing part of the site's editorial visual identity (it reinforces the Fraunces/Hanken Grotesk/Space Mono type system), so a blanket removal is not obviously correct — the tell is in the uniform repetition and genericness, not in the existence of a kicker per se.
goal: Keep the wayfinding and typographic-identity value a kicker/eyebrow provides (a small mono accent that signals 'this is EnsureCollege, and here is what this block is about') while removing the specific signature that reads as AI-templated: identical uppercase tracked labels stamped above every single section regardless of whether they add information. A modern, human-authored treatment should (1) reserve the label for places where it names something real and specific rather than restating the obvious, (2) vary the heading treatment across the page instead of applying one component uniformly, and (3) borrow from how actual magazines/newspapers use kickers — as a specific, editorially-considered piece of copy (a rubric, dateline, or category that could not be swapped between sections without changing its meaning) rather than a decorative shape that happens to contain words.

options.option: Real editorial kicker (specific rubric/category, not decorative filler)
options.description: Keep the small mono label, but make its copy do real, section-specific work the way print kickers do: a rubric name, a topic tag, or a one-line frame that could not be pasted onto a different section unchanged (e.g. 'REPORTING', 'Q&A', 'THE DATA', or a named track like 'FINANCIAL AID' rather than a generic 'OUR APPROACH'/'WHY IT WORKS'). Drop it anywhere it would just restate the heading.
options.why_it_reads_human: Human editors write kickers as classification decisions tied to actual content structure (this piece is a Q&A, that one is a data story); AI-slop eyebrows are near-synonyms of the heading repeated for rhythm. Specificity and irregularity (some sections get one, some don't, wording differs in kind not just topic) signal editorial judgment rather than a component that got dropped into a loop.
options.adoption_examples: NYT Magazine and Bloomberg Businessweek digital features use rubric-style kickers ('The Sunday Read', 'Etc.') that name a recurring franchise, not a paraphrase of the headline; The Atlantic and The Guardian use section/topic eyebrows (e.g. 'Health', 'Opinion') that are genuine taxonomy, reused across many articles rather than invented per-block.

options.description: Replace the text eyebrow with a sequence marker using a CSS counter, positioning the site's content as an ordered set of steps or chapters.
options.why_it_reads_human: Numbering can feel intentional and structured (a real sequence, like a syllabus or a report's table of contents) when the sections genuinely form a progression a reader moves through in order.
options.adoption_examples: Stripe's docs and some product marketing pages ('01 Connect', '02 Configure') use this for genuinely sequential setup flows; long-form magazine features (Pitchfork, The Verge feature templates) use chapter numerals for multi-part narratives.

options.option: Inline / run-in kicker (same line as the heading, not stacked above it)
options.description: Fold the label into the heading line itself, e.g. small-caps mono prefix followed by an em dash or pipe, then the heading in the display serif: 'FINANCIAL AID — Every dollar mapped.' No separate stacked line.
options.why_it_reads_human: Removes the single most template-recognizable silhouette (small label / big headline, stacked, centered or left-aligned identically every time) while keeping the type-pairing accent. Forces the label to be short since it now competes for the same line, which naturally curbs generic filler text.
options.adoption_examples: Craig Mod's newsletter and several independent editorial sites (Are.na blog, some Kottke-adjacent designs) run a small mono/caps tag inline before a title on the same baseline rather than as a separate stacked row.

options.option: Rule / divider treatment (no label text at all)
options.description: Mark section starts with a plain rule (a thin border-top, a short accent-colored tick mark, or generous whitespace) instead of a text label. Hierarchy comes from spacing, rule weight, and the heading's own size/color rather than a caption.
options.why_it_reads_human: This is the classic Swiss/international typographic-style move (Müller-Brockmann-derived grid systems): let structure and whitespace signal a new section, not a caption every time. It reads as confident restraint rather than a component library default.
options.adoption_examples: Many documentation and editorial sites built on strict grid systems (early Medium templates, A Book Apart's site, Vercel's own blog in places) use a rule or increased top-margin plus a color shift instead of a text eyebrow.

options.option: No eyebrow — direct, standalone heading only
options.description: Remove the label entirely for most sections; let the Fraunces heading stand alone, sized and weighted to carry its own hierarchy. Optionally reserve a metadata line (byline/dateline-style, e.g. a date or read-time) only where it is factually meaningful, not decorative.
options.why_it_reads_human: A confident, well-set standalone heading is what most non-templated editorial and product sites actually do most of the time; the eyebrow-on-everything pattern is the exception in professional design, not the rule. Removing it where it adds nothing is the lowest-risk fix.
options.adoption_examples: Basecamp's marketing pages, Linear's changelog, and most Apple product pages use large standalone headings for the majority of sections, reserving any small label for the one or two places (e.g. a press quote attribution) where it is informationally necessary.

options.option: Selective/mixed use across the page (deliberate inconsistency)
options.description: Apply a different one of the above four treatments to different sections of the same page based on what each section actually needs — hero keeps a kicker (or drops it for a dateline-style line), a data section gets a rule, a narrative section gets no label, a sequential section gets numbering.
options.why_it_reads_human: Real editorial layouts are not stamped from one component; a magazine spread varies treatment by article type. Deliberate variation, if it maps to real structural differences (not randomness), is the strongest signal of human editorial decision-making and directly answers the 'uniform repetition' tell.
options.adoption_examples: Print magazines route-decide this per feature type as a matter of course (a profile gets a kicker + dek, a Q&A gets a numbered/lettered format, a photo essay gets none); digital analogues appear on sites like The Outline (archived) and Bloomberg Businessweek's feature templates, which visibly change heading treatment by story format.

recommended: A mixed approach (option 6, built from options 1, 4 and 5): keep the mono eyebrow only in the hero and at most one or two sections where the label names something real and specific (e.g. a named program track), rewritten so it could not be pasted onto another section unchanged; convert the remaining repeated generic eyebrows to a plain rule/whitespace break (option 4) or drop them to a standalone heading (option 5); reserve numbering (option 2) only for sections that are a genuine sequence (e.g. a 3-step application process), since per impeccable.style's own analysis numbered markers are 'the AI editorial scaffold one tier deeper' and would recreate the same tell in a different shape if used everywhere. This keeps the Space Mono/Fraunces type pairing — the site's actual identity asset — visible in the hero and in a couple of well-chosen spots, while eliminating the uniform, decorative repetition that reads as templated. For a light-only editorial site with a marigold accent and a professional/dashboard-leaning owner, this reads as an edited publication (varied treatment, considered copy) rather than a generated one (one component, looped).
trend_context: 2025-2026 design commentary has specifically named the tiny-uppercase-tracked-label-over-headline pattern as a default AI-SaaS/AI-editorial signature (impeccable.style calls out both the single hero eyebrow/pill-chip and the repeated section-kicker variant as tells, and flags numbered '01/02/03' markers as the next-tier version of the same scaffold). The countertrend among sites trying to read as deliberately-designed in this period is (a) heading treatments that vary by section/content-type rather than one repeated component, (b) a return to rule-and-whitespace-driven hierarchy (Swiss/International Typographic Style revival) instead of captioning every block, and (c) kickers that survive only where they carry real, specific editorial information (a rubric, a franchise name, a dateline) rather than generic restatement. Established editorial-design conventions (print kickers, magazine eyebrows) still support the underlying pattern; what has shifted is that its uniform, decorative, present-on-every-block application is now legible as templated/generated rather than edited.
vanilla_css_notes:
Kicker as its own element, not inside the heading: mark the label as a sibling

/ immediately before or after the

in source order, per Sebastian Greger's guidance that the kicker semantically follows/supports the heading rather than nesting inside it; use CSS (flex-direction: column-reverse, or simple source-order + margin) to control visual stacking independent of DOM order if you want it to render above the heading while following it logically. For the rule/divider option: `.section-heading{border-top:1px solid var(--rule-color);padding-top:1rem}` or a short accent tick via `::before{content:'';display:block;width:2.5rem;height:2px;background:var(--marigold);margin-bottom:.75rem}` — no JS required. For inline kicker: put label and heading in one flex/inline-flex container on one baseline, e.g. `.heading-row{display:flex;align-items:baseline;gap:.5rem}` with the label in Space Mono uppercase (`font-family:var(--mono);text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:.05em;font-size:.75rem`) and the heading in Fraunces. For numbered sections, use CSS counters — no inline script needed, satisfying the CSP script-src 'self' constraint: set `body{counter-reset:section}` and on each `.section{counter-increment:section}` with `.section .kicker::before{content:counter(section,decimal-leading-zero) ' — '}`; this is pure CSS, so no external JS file is required at all for numbering. Only reach for an external static JS file if you want numbering to depend on runtime/DOM conditions (e.g. skipping hidden sections) that CSS counters can't express reliably.
accessibility_notes:
Per WCAG 2.4.10 (Section Headings), the kicker/eyebrow text itself should not be marked up as its own heading level (don't make it an

that outranks or sits oddly beside the real

) — treat it as a

or labeled visually, so the document's heading outline stays clean for screen-reader users navigating by heading. If the label is purely decorative/redundant with the heading text, consider `aria-hidden="true"` on it and let the heading alone be announced; if it carries unique information (a real rubric), leave it exposed to the accessibility tree normally. Because eyebrow text is typically small (11-13px) and tracked/uppercase, verify it still meets WCAG AA contrast (4.5:1 for text under ~18px/24px bold) against the background — marigold-on-white or muted-gray-on-white eyebrow treatments often fail this at small sizes and need darkening. Uppercase + wide letter-spacing can also affect screen-reader pronunciation of some words/acronyms; test with VoiceOver/NVDA or provide the underlying text in normal case with `text-transform:uppercase` purely as a visual effect (this preserves correct pronunciation since text-transform doesn't change the DOM text). Respect `prefers-reduced-motion` if any label reveal is animated on scroll — fall back to no animation, not a jump-cut.
pitfalls: Numbered sections (01/02/03) can become their own AI-scaffold tell if applied uniformly to non-sequential content — reserve them for genuinely ordered content (steps, chapters), not as a decorative substitute for the eyebrow. A rule/divider used identically before every section recreates the same 'one component, looped' problem in a different form; vary rule weight/spacing or omit it in some places too. Inline kickers can clutter the heading line and hurt readability/wrapping on mobile if the label is too long — keep it to one or two words. Dropping the eyebrow everywhere risks losing real wayfinding value in a genuinely long or complex page (e.g. a scholarship-matching tool with many distinct functional zones); some orientation label may still be needed for usability, just not decoratively repeated. Deliberate variation across sections (option 6) must map to actual structural/content differences — variation applied at random or 'for visual interest' alone reads as arbitrary rather than edited, which is its own tell once a reader notices there's no underlying logic.
